courseMetadata.title: Basic Adult Skills
courseMetadata.description: A practical, intermediate-level course for mid-to-late-20-somethings covering the financial, interpersonal, and administrative skills adult life requires but rarely teaches directly: budgeting and banking, credit and debt, communication and negotiation, taxes and bureaucratic navigation, health and insurance, housing decisions, home and vehicle maintenance, working with contractors, and long-term planning. Every section pairs concrete worked examples with cross-domain transfer exercises so skills learned in one context (e.g., negotiating a price) are explicitly re-applied in another (e.g., a lease renewal or a contractor quote). Note: tax, insurance, credit, and housing content is written for a United States context; learners elsewhere should treat the specific institutions, thresholds, and legal rules as illustrative.

prerequisites.description: Basic reading comprehension and everyday arithmetic (percentages, simple multiplication and division).
No specific financial or legal background is required; having a recent pay stub or bank statement on hand is helpful but optional for the practical activities.

interleavingGroups.rationaleNote: Contrasts the same anchor/BATNA/trade-off and SIR structures across three surface domains (roommate/price negotiation in Sec 2, landlord renewal in Sec 6, contractor/vehicle negotiation in Sec 8) to force near-transfer rather than domain-bound memorization.

Reuses the same escalation-ladder structure first taught via a tax/billing dispute (Sec 4) and then applied to a denied insurance claim (Sec 5), testing genuine transfer of an abstract procedure across two different bureaucracies.
Interleaves the research-attempt-escalate framework (Sec 7, home issues) with vehicle warning-light diagnostics (Sec 8) so learners practice the same triage logic on a mechanically different system.

Repeatedly surfaces the same time-value-of-money reasoning across an emergency fund (Sec 1), a mortgage affordability calculation (Sec 6), a vehicle loan comparison (Sec 8), and an employer-match calculation (Sec 9), each at increasing stakes and decreasing scaffolding.
Forces integrated recall of credit, debt, and insurance competencies together in the Section 9 capstone synthesis exercise, rather than testing each in isolation as in Sections 3 and 5.

Places resilience-reframing immediately after a sequence of failure-prone real-world tasks (negotiating, appealing, applying) so the reframe has recent concrete experience to anchor to, and contrasts it with the difficult-conversations competency's handling of interpersonal friction.

probeQuestions.prompt: If your take-home pay covers all your bills with money left over, do you still need a budget?
options: No, a budget is only for people overspending their income.
Yes, because without a plan it's hard to see where the surplus is actually going or whether it's serving your goals.
No, budgets are only useful for restricting spending.
Only if you have debt.

optionFeedback.feedback: This is a common assumption, but it overlooks the value of visibility into surplus spending.
This is correct - a budget's job is visibility and allocation, not just crisis management.
A budget is an allocation plan, not purely a restriction tool.
Budgets are useful regardless of debt status.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: Roughly how much more would $5,000 earn in one year in a 4.5% APY HYSA compared to a checking account paying 0.01% APY?
options: About $2
About $22
About $225
About $2,250

optionFeedback.feedback: This understates the gap; recompute 5000 x 0.045 vs 5000 x 0.0001.
This still understates the gap by a factor of 10.
Correct - roughly $225 in extra interest for simply choosing a different account.
This overstates the gap by a factor of 10.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: Does closing an old, unused credit card typically help or hurt your credit score?
options: It helps, since fewer open accounts looks more responsible.
It typically hurts, since it reduces available credit (raising utilization) and can shorten average account age.
It has no effect either way.
It only matters if the card has an annual fee.

optionFeedback.feedback: This is a common assumption, but the utilization-ratio math shows the opposite effect.
Correct - this is exactly why closing old cards is often discouraged.
It affects two of the five scoring factors: utilization and account age.
The fee isn't the relevant factor; the utilization and account-age effects apply regardless of fees.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: If a raise pushes part of your income into a higher tax bracket, what happens to your take-home pay?
options: It decreases, since your whole income is now taxed at the higher rate.
It always increases, since only the portion of income within the new bracket is taxed at the higher rate.
It stays exactly the same.
It depends on your employer.

optionFeedback.feedback: This describes a flat-tax misunderstanding; the U.S. system taxes marginally.
Correct - take-home pay never drops from earning more under a marginal system.
Take-home pay does increase with a raise, just by less than the full raise amount.
This isn't determined by the employer; it's a function of the tax bracket structure.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: Does a landlord's insurance policy typically cover a tenant's personal belongings if they're stolen or destroyed?
options: Yes, automatically.
No - landlord policies generally cover the building itself; tenant belongings require separate renters insurance.
Only if the tenant has lived there over a year.
Only for damage caused by the landlord's negligence.

optionFeedback.feedback: Landlord policies typically cover the building structure, not tenant possessions.
Correct - this coverage gap is exactly what renters insurance is designed to close.
There's no such tenure-based rule.
This isn't the relevant distinction; the policy simply doesn't cover tenant possessions regardless of cause.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: Is comparing only the monthly mortgage payment to monthly rent a fair way to decide between renting and buying?
options: Yes, that's the only cost that matters.
No - closing costs, PMI, property tax, insurance, and maintenance reserves add substantially to the true cost of buying.
Yes, as long as the home is under $300,000.
No, because renting is always cheaper regardless of horizon.

optionFeedback.feedback: This ignores several major costs that materially change the comparison.
Correct - a fair comparison requires the full cost stack, not just the mortgage payment.
Price threshold isn't the relevant factor; the missing costs apply regardless of home price.
This overstates the case; the horizon and full cost picture determine which is cheaper, not a blanket rule.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: A small ceiling stain hasn't visibly grown in a few days. Is it safe to ignore?
options: Yes, if it's not growing it's not urgent.
Not necessarily - the size of a visible symptom isn't a reliable guide to how urgent the underlying cause is.
Yes, ceiling stains are always just condensation.
No, it should always be treated as an emergency requiring immediate wall removal.

optionFeedback.feedback: A slow leak can cause hidden damage over months even while looking minor on the surface.
Correct - the underlying cause needs investigating regardless of the stain's visible size.
Roof leaks and plumbing leaks are equally plausible causes that shouldn't be assumed away.
The framework starts with research and a safe, accessible check, not immediately opening a wall.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: A car's oil pressure warning light comes on but the car still seems to drive fine. What should you do?
options: Keep driving normally since it feels fine.
Stop driving as soon as it's safely possible - this is a red-tier warning that can precede severe engine damage.
Address it within the next few weeks.
Ignore it unless a mechanic flags it at the next routine visit.

optionFeedback.feedback: Drives fine isn't a reliable signal for red-tier warnings.
Correct - red-tier warnings like oil pressure require immediate attention.
Red-tier warnings shouldn't be deferred for convenience.
Waiting for a routine visit risks the exact failure the warning is meant to prevent.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: An employer matches 50% of 401(k) contributions up to 6% of salary. An employee contributes only 3%. What does this represent?
options: A negligible difference not worth adjusting.
Walking away from guaranteed employer money that also loses decades of compound growth if left uncaptured.
A decision that only matters closer to retirement age.
Something with no real dollar impact since it's a small percentage.

optionFeedback.feedback: The compounded impact over decades is substantial, not negligible.
Correct - this is effectively giving up a guaranteed return plus decades of growth.
The cost compounds precisely because it isn't captured early; it doesn't only matter later.
The percentage sounds small, but the compounded dollar impact is significant.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: Is negotiating a price or contract terms generally an inappropriate thing to do?
options: Yes, it's rude to ask for different terms than what's offered.
No - negotiation is a standard, expected part of many transactions, and a structured approach makes it collaborative rather than adversarial.
Only acceptable for large business deals.
Only acceptable if you're willing to be aggressive about it.

optionFeedback.feedback: Negotiation is a normal, expected part of many transactions.
Correct - anchoring, trade-offs, and a walk-away point can make negotiation collaborative.
The same negotiation logic applies at any transaction size.
A respectful, structured approach works without requiring aggression.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as prior mastery signal for the linked competency.

competencies.title: Personal Budgeting & Cash Flow
competencies.description: The ability to build, maintain, and adjust a personal budget that allocates income across needs, wants, savings, and debt paydown.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can construct a complete monthly budget from real income and expense figures and correctly re-allocate it after a change in circumstances.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Budgeting means restriction and deprivation.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A budget is an allocation plan that assigns a purpose to every dollar, including planned discretionary spending; it is not a spending cap on everything.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner build a budget that explicitly includes a funded 'wants' category, then reflect on how that differs from a restriction list.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: If income comfortably covers bills, a budget is unnecessary.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Without a budget, surplus income still leaks to low-priority spending with no visibility into where it goes or whether it serves the learner's actual goals.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner track one month of actual discretionary spending against no plan, then compare it to a planned allocation.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A budget is a one-time setup that doesn't need revisiting.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Income, expenses, and goals change over time (raises, moves, new debts), so a budget needs periodic revision to stay accurate.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Prompt a quarterly budget-review habit tied to a calendar reminder, and show a worked example of a budget becoming stale after a rent increase.

competencies.title: Banking Products & Fee Structures
competencies.description: Understanding account types (checking, savings, HYSA), how they're used, and how fee structures affect money left in them.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can select an appropriate account type for a stated goal and calculate the real cost of common banking fees.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: All banks and account types are functionally interchangeable.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Interest rates (APY), fee structures, and minimum balance requirements vary widely across banks and account types, and these differences compound significantly over time.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner compare their own bank's savings APY against a real HYSA offer and calculate the annual dollar difference.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Overdraft protection functions as a free short-term loan.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Overdraft coverage typically carries a flat fee per incident that can exceed the amount overdrawn, making it an extremely expensive form of borrowing, not free credit.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the worked example of a $15 overdraft triggering a $35 fee, and discuss linking a savings account as a free backup transfer instead.

competencies.title: Compound Growth & Delayed Gratification
competencies.description: Understanding and applying the logic of compound growth and delayed gratification to weigh short-term versus long-term financial and personal tradeoffs.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can calculate and explain the effect of starting earlier on a compounding outcome, and transfer that reasoning to a non-financial scenario.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Small amounts saved or invested early don't matter much.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Compound growth makes time the dominant variable; an early small contribution can outgrow a later larger one because it has more time to compound.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Run the 'start at 25 vs. start at 35' worked comparison with the learner's own contribution amount using a compound interest calculator.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Long-horizon thinking means sacrificing all present enjoyment.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: It means deliberately balancing short-term and long-term priorities, not eliminating short-term spending entirely; a budget can fund both.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Point back to the budgeting-cashflow competency's funded 'wants' category as proof the two aren't mutually exclusive.

competencies.title: Negotiation & Contractual Communication
competencies.description: Applying negotiation techniques - anchoring, trade-offs, and walk-away points - in price, terms, and service-related conversations.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can plan and execute a structured negotiation, including a walk-away point, in a novel domain.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Negotiating a price or terms is rude or inappropriate.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Negotiation is a standard, expected part of many transactions (rentals, contractor work, used goods); a structured, respectful approach makes it collaborative, not confrontational.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner practice a scripted negotiation opener and get feedback on tone rather than avoiding the interaction.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A verbal agreement is just as reliable as a written one.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A written agreement creates a verifiable record that protects both parties if there's a later dispute about what was actually agreed.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a worked example of a verbal-only agreement leading to a disputed outcome, contrasted with the same scenario documented in writing.

competencies.title: Hard Interpersonal Conversations
competencies.description: Applying a structured communication approach (Situation-Impact-Request) to initiate and navigate hard conversations with roommates, partners, bosses, or landlords.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can plan a difficult conversation using the SIR framework in a novel relational context.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Avoiding a difficult conversation avoids the conflict.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Unaddressed issues typically resurface later, often bigger and with more accumulated frustration than if addressed early.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner map a real recurring issue they've been avoiding and predict how it likely grows if left unaddressed for another month.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Being direct is the same as being harsh.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Assertive communication states a need clearly while still respecting the other person; aggressive communication does so by blaming or attacking - clarity and respect can coexist.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner rewrite an aggressive script into an assertive one using the SIR framework, keeping the same underlying need.

competencies.title: Maintaining Adult Friendships
competencies.description: Recognizing and applying intentional practices for maintaining adult friendships once shared institutional structures (like school) disappear.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can design a concrete, recurring plan for maintaining an adult friendship.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Good friendships maintain themselves without effort.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Adult life removes the passive daily proximity (school, dorms) that sustained friendships automatically; without that structure, someone has to initiate deliberately.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner identify one friendship that's faded and name the shared structure that used to maintain it without effort.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Reaching out first seems needy or imposing.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Most people are relieved when someone else initiates contact, and this fear is a mismatch between assumption and reality that causes people to under-initiate.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner send one low-stakes outreach message this week and note the actual response versus their predicted one.

competencies.title: Credit Scores & Credit Reports
competencies.description: Interpreting credit scores and reports, and understanding which actions raise or lower them.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can read a credit report/score and correctly explain which specific actions raise or lower it.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Carrying a balance (not paying in full) improves your credit score.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Utilization, not carried interest, affects the score; paying in full each month does not hurt it and avoids interest entirely.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show the utilization-ratio math directly and clarify that scoring doesn't reward paying interest.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Checking your own credit report lowers your score.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Self-checks are soft inquiries and don't affect the score; only lender-initiated hard inquiries have a small, temporary effect.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Clarify the hard-vs-soft inquiry distinction and encourage regular free self-monitoring.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Closing an old, unused credit card can only help your score.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Closing a card reduces total available credit (raising the utilization ratio even if the balance is unchanged) and can shorten average account age - both can lower the score.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the before/after utilization-ratio calculation from the worked example.

competencies.title: Debt Prioritization & Payoff Strategy
competencies.description: Evaluating and sequencing debt payoff using avalanche (highest interest first) or snowball (smallest balance first) methods.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can construct a debt payoff plan using either method and justify the choice based on math vs. behavior, including in a transfer context.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: All debt is equally harmful regardless of interest rate.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Interest rates vary enormously (a 22% APR credit card vs. a 5% APR student loan), which changes both urgency and the total cost of carrying each debt.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner compute total interest cost across debts at different rates to see the disparity directly.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Making minimum payments represents meaningful progress toward payoff.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Minimum payments on high-interest debt are structured so a large share goes to interest, barely reducing principal.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show an amortization-style worked example of how little principal a minimum payment actually reduces on a high-APR balance.

competencies.title: Reading Contracts & Avoiding Predatory Terms
competencies.description: Reading fine print and identifying predatory lending or subscription/contract traps such as BNPL, payday loans, and auto-renewals.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can analyze a real contract for hidden costs and cancellation friction, including in a non-financial contract.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Buy now, pay later is basically free since there's no stated interest.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Missed payments often trigger flat late fees or convert the entire remaining balance to a high interest rate retroactively, so the 'free' financing is contingent on perfect timing.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show the worked example of a missed BNPL payment and the resulting fee/interest conversion.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: If a service is easy to sign up for, cancellation will be just as easy.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Many services intentionally design cancellation to be more difficult than signup - a known dark pattern - to reduce cancellations.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner locate a real subscription's cancellation flow and count the number of steps compared to signup.

competencies.title: How Income Tax Works
competencies.description: Explaining how progressive tax brackets, marginal vs. effective rate, and withholding determine take-home pay.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can correctly compute and explain marginal vs. effective rate, and transfer that reasoning to a non-tax scenario.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Moving into a higher tax bracket lowers your overall take-home pay.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: The U.S. system is marginal - only the income within each bracket is taxed at that bracket's rate, so take-home pay always rises with a raise, never falls.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the full bracket-by-bracket worked calculation on a $50,000 example, showing marginal vs. effective rate side by side.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A large tax refund means you did taxes well.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A refund means too much was withheld throughout the year, effectively an interest-free loan to the government rather than a financial win.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show how adjusting W-4 withholding could redirect that money into a HYSA earning interest for the taxpayer instead.

competencies.title: Filing Taxes Accurately
competencies.description: Applying the steps to file taxes accurately: gathering forms, choosing filing status, distinguishing deductions from credits, and meeting deadlines.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can correctly distinguish deductions from credits and correctly determine what income must be reported.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Tax deductions and tax credits work the same way.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A deduction reduces taxable income (its value depends on your marginal rate), while a credit reduces the tax owed directly, dollar-for-dollar - a credit of the same size is worth more.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Run the $1,000-deduction-vs-$1,000-credit worked example at a specific marginal rate to show the dollar gap directly.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Freelance or gig income under some vague threshold doesn't need to be reported.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Nearly all income is reportable regardless of amount; a payer not being required to issue a 1099 below a threshold only affects the payer's obligation, not the recipient's.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Clarify the distinction between payer-reporting thresholds and the taxpayer's own reporting obligation.

competencies.title: Escalating & Appealing Within Bureaucracies
competencies.description: Applying strategies to escalate, document, and appeal issues within bureaucratic systems such as government agencies, insurers, and customer service.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can apply a documented escalation strategy to resolve a disputed issue, including in an unfamiliar institutional context.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The first answer given by a representative is final.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Front-line representatives are often not empowered or motivated to resolve unusual cases; escalation to a supervisor or a written appeal frequently produces a different outcome.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the escalation-ladder worked example and have the learner identify the next rung for a real unresolved issue.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Rules, fees, and deadlines are always fixed with no exceptions.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Many policies have documented exception or waiver processes that a first answer doesn't reflect, especially when requested through the correct channel.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner search for a specific policy's stated exception/appeal process before assuming none exists.

competencies.title: Insurance Coverage Evaluation
competencies.description: Evaluating insurance options (health, renters, auto) against a personal risk profile, and interpreting policy documents and EOBs.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can evaluate insurance options against a personal risk profile and correctly interpret coverage documents.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Insurance is wasted money if you never file a claim.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Insurance's value is risk transfer and protection against catastrophic loss, not a savings product that must 'pay out' to be worthwhile.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Reframe insurance explicitly as the cost of transferring low-probability, high-severity risk, using a concrete catastrophic-loss example.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A landlord's insurance policy covers a renter's personal belongings.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Landlord policies typically cover the building structure, not tenant possessions, which require separate renters insurance.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the worked example of an $8,000 personal-property loss with no renters insurance.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The cheapest premium is automatically the best policy.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A lower premium is often paired with a higher deductible or narrower coverage, which can raise out-of-pocket risk substantially.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Compare two plans with different premium/deductible combinations against the same claim scenario to show the trade-off.

competencies.title: Choosing Care & Understanding Coverage
competencies.description: Applying strategies to choose healthcare providers, understand insurance coverage before care, and recognize when to seek medical or mental health support.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can select an appropriate care option and correctly reason about network-based cost differences.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A visit costs the same regardless of network, under the same insurance plan.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Out-of-network providers have no negotiated rate with the insurer and can bill much higher amounts, which the plan covers at a lower percentage.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the worked in-network ($40) vs. out-of-network ($250+) cost comparison for the same visit type.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Mental health concerns only warrant professional help once they become severe.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Early or moderate-level support is typically more effective, and accessible options (EAPs, sliding-scale providers) exist specifically for non-crisis levels of concern.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Reframe therapy/counseling as preventive care, similar to a routine physical, rather than a crisis-only resource.

competencies.title: Rent-vs-Buy & Affordability Analysis
competencies.description: Evaluating rent-vs-buy tradeoffs, total cost of homeownership, and affordability thresholds like debt-to-income ratio.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can calculate a full rent-vs-buy or lease-vs-buy comparison and a personal affordability ceiling, including in a transfer context.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Renting is always throwing money away compared to buying.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Renting purchases flexibility and shifts maintenance/ownership risk to the landlord; the true cost comparison depends on the full financial picture, not just the payment amount.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the full 5-year total-cost worked comparison, including closing costs, PMI, taxes, and maintenance reserves.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A mortgage pre-approval amount is what you should plan to spend.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Pre-approval reflects the lender's risk tolerance for how much they'll lend, not the borrower's own budget priorities and goals.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner calculate their own DTI-based affordability ceiling and compare it against a hypothetical pre-approval amount.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The sale price is the only real cost of buying a home.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Closing costs, PMI, property tax, insurance, and ongoing maintenance reserves add substantially to the true cost beyond the sale price.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Break down the worked example's monthly cost stack line by line, from mortgage P&I to maintenance reserve.

competencies.title: Reading & Negotiating Lease Agreements
competencies.description: Analyzing lease agreements for tenant rights, obligations, and negotiable or red-flag clauses.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can identify red-flag and negotiable clauses in a lease, and transfer that skill to a different type of contract.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A security deposit is automatically non-refundable.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Most jurisdictions require an itemized list of any deductions, with the remainder returned within a set timeframe.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner look up their own jurisdiction's security-deposit-return rules and timeframe.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Every clause in a lease is fixed and non-negotiable before signing.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Move-in dates, minor fees, and renewal terms are often negotiable before signing, even though they appear as fixed print on the page.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner identify one clause in a sample lease they would attempt to negotiate and draft an opening line.

competencies.title: Home Systems Maintenance & Safety
competencies.description: Applying routine maintenance tasks and safety practices across home systems (HVAC, plumbing, electrical, fire safety).

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can build a maintenance checklist and correctly judge urgency and DIY-vs-professional boundaries for a home issue.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: If nothing looks visibly broken, no maintenance is needed.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Many systems (HVAC filters, water heaters, slow leaks) degrade invisibly and fail without preventive care, regardless of visible symptoms.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the ceiling-stain worked example showing a small visible symptom masking a larger, ongoing problem.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Any online DIY tutorial makes a repair safe and legal to attempt.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Tutorials often omit safety, legal, or code requirements specific to a given situation, especially for electrical, gas, or structural work.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Contrast a low-risk DIY task (replacing a switch cover) with a high-risk one (breaker panel work) and explain why licensing exists for the latter.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A small, slow leak isn't urgent because it looks minor.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A slow leak can cause escalating structural damage and mold over months, even while the visible symptom stays small.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Reiterate that visible symptom size isn't a reliable proxy for underlying urgency.

competencies.title: Research-Attempt-Escalate Framework
competencies.description: Applying a general framework - research possible causes, attempt a safe fix within skill/legal limits, escalate to a professional - to unfamiliar practical problems.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can apply the research-attempt-escalate framework to an unfamiliar problem outside the home-maintenance domain.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: If an online tutorial exists for a task, it's safe to follow.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Tutorials often omit safety, legal, or code requirements specific to the learner's exact situation, especially for electrical, gas, or structural work.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner identify one task where a tutorial might miss a jurisdiction-specific permit or code requirement.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Calling a professional immediately signals a failure of self-sufficiency.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Recognizing the limits of one's own skill and escalating appropriately is the correct completion of the framework, not a failure of it.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Reframe 'escalate' explicitly as a successful outcome of the framework, not a fallback admission of defeat.

competencies.title: Routine Vehicle Maintenance & Diagnostics
competencies.description: Applying a routine maintenance schedule and interpreting dashboard warning indicators by urgency tier.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can correctly interpret dashboard warnings by urgency and follow a maintenance schedule appropriately.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: If a car drives fine, maintenance can wait.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Many failures (brakes wearing down, timing components) are undetectable by feel until they are severe or dangerous, so 'drives fine' isn't a reliable signal.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Contrast a symptom-based check with a schedule-based check and explain why some failures give no advance warning through feel.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Dashboard warning lights can be addressed whenever convenient.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Red-tier lights (oil pressure, temperature) indicate imminent risk of severe damage if driving continues, unlike lower-urgency yellow-tier lights.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the worked example of an ignored oil-pressure light leading to an engine replacement.

competencies.title: Total Cost of Vehicle Ownership
competencies.description: Evaluating total cost of ownership for a vehicle, including financing, insurance, depreciation, and maintenance.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can calculate total cost of ownership across financing scenarios and transfer that reasoning to a different purchase type.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The monthly payment is the main thing that matters in a financing decision.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A lower monthly payment can come from a longer loan term that costs substantially more in total interest over the life of the loan.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Run the 4-year-vs-6-year loan worked comparison to show the total interest gap directly.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A longer loan term always saves money.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Longer terms typically combine a higher interest rate with more months of interest accrual, increasing total cost despite the smaller monthly payment.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner recompute total interest for a loan term of their choosing to see the pattern generalize.

competencies.title: Vetting Contractors & Service Providers
competencies.description: Evaluating and selecting qualified contractors or service providers using licensing, documentation, and reputation signals rather than price alone.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can evaluate multiple service-provider quotes using criteria beyond price, including in a non-contractor domain.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The lowest quote is automatically the best deal.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: An unusually low bid, especially paired with red flags like cash-only payment or no written estimate, often signals corner-cutting, missing licensure, or a bait-and-switch.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the three-quote worked comparison showing why the cheapest option carried the most risk.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Any handyman or contractor can legally perform any type of repair.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Certain categories of work - electrical, plumbing, gas, some structural work - often legally require a licensed and sometimes permitted professional.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner look up their state's licensing requirements for one specific trade (e.g., electrical work).

competencies.title: Retirement Accounts & Compound Growth
competencies.description: Understanding retirement account types and applying compound-growth reasoning to the value of an employer match and early contributions.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can calculate the cost of an uncaptured employer match and correctly distinguish retirement account types.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Retirement saving can safely wait until income is higher.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Compound growth makes early contributions disproportionately valuable versus later, larger ones; the uncaptured time cannot be recovered later at the same value.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Run the uncaptured-employer-match worked example showing the ~$85,000 30-year cost of contributing 3% instead of 6%.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A 401(k) and an IRA are essentially the same thing.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: They differ in sponsorship (employer vs. independent), contribution limits, and employer-match eligibility, even when both come in Traditional and Roth versions.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner look up the current contribution limits for each account type and compare them side by side.

competencies.title: Major Purchases & Life-Admin Systems
competencies.description: Evaluating major purchases using a structured cost-benefit framework, and assembling basic life-admin documents (will, power of attorney, records).

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can evaluate a major purchase using total cost and alternatives, and assemble a basic life-admin document system.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A young, healthy person doesn't need a will or power of attorney.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Incapacity or death can occur unexpectedly at any age, and without these documents, default state rules - not necessarily personal wishes - determine outcomes.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner look up their own state's intestacy default rules to see how they compare to their actual wishes.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A major purchase should be evaluated on sticker price alone.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: True total cost (including financing interest), opportunity cost of that money elsewhere, and alignment with existing budget priorities all affect whether a purchase makes sense.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Run the structured cost-benefit worked example on a $3,000 trip, including financing interest and opportunity cost.

competencies.title: Processing Rejection & Setbacks
competencies.description: Applying healthy reframing strategies to process rejection and setbacks (job, housing, relationship) without derailing progress.

desiredOutcomes.description: Learner can apply resilience-reframing techniques to a personal setback and transfer them to a professional/organizational one.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Rejection at this life stage reflects personal inadequacy.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Rejection in jobs, apartments, and dating is a normal, largely numbers-driven part of adult life - a competitive process filtering many applicants down to one produces many more rejections than acceptances for everyone, including strong candidates.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner estimate the realistic ratio of applications-to-offers for their situation to reframe rejection as an expected data point.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Resilience means not feeling upset by setbacks.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Resilience means continuing to act despite difficult emotions, not suppressing or avoiding them.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Explicitly separate 'processing the emotion' from 'continuing to act' as two distinct, both-necessary steps.

sections.title: Money Foundations: Budgeting & Banking
sections.description: The opening section of the course. Learners build a real monthly budget, learn how bank account types and fees actually work, and see the mathematics of compound growth applied to both savings and emergency-fund planning. Every later section assumes this foundation is solid.

learningObjectives.description: Construct a monthly budget that allocates income across needs, wants, savings, and debt paydown.
Differentiate checking, savings, and high-yield savings accounts, and select an appropriate structure for a stated goal.
Calculate the one-year cost impact of common banking fees.
Calculate and compare the outcome of starting to save at two different ages, illustrating compound growth.
Design an emergency fund plan with a target amount and a realistic savings timeline.

motivationHooks.prompt: A 10-year difference in when you start saving can be worth hundreds of thousands of dollars by retirement - not because of how much you save, but because of when. This section is the one financial habit that compounds into every other decision in this course.

Before you see the numbers: if two people save the same amount every month but one starts 10 years earlier, how much bigger do you think their final balance will be - a little bigger, or dramatically bigger? Hold that guess in mind.

Every section after this one - renting an apartment, financing a car, choosing insurance - eventually comes down to 'can my budget absorb this?' Getting budgeting and banking right here makes every later decision easier.

furtherReading.rationaleNote: Free, plain-language budgeting worksheets and tools from the U.S. consumer protection regulator.

furtherReading.author: Vicki Robin

furtherReading.rationaleNote: A foundational, values-based approach to budgeting that goes beyond spreadsheets.

furtherReading.title: I Will Teach You to Be Rich
furtherReading.author: Ramit Sethi
