questions.prompt: Explain the core difference between a Traditional and a Roth retirement account, and describe one situation where choosing Roth might make more sense than Traditional.
successCriteria: Response correctly applies the core reasoning to the specific scenario given, not just a restatement of the general rule.
Response reaches a concrete, specific conclusion rather than a vague generality.

rubric.excellent: Correctly explains the pre-tax-now/taxed-later vs. taxed-now/tax-free-later distinction, and gives a coherent situation (e.g., expecting to be in a higher tax bracket in retirement, or early career with a currently low tax rate) for preferring Roth.
rubric.adequate: Correctly explains the tax-timing distinction but gives a vague or unclear rationale for when Roth might be preferable.
rubric.weak: Treats the two account types as interchangeable or can't articulate the tax-timing difference.

errorPatterns.description: Learner conflates Traditional and Roth accounts as functionally identical.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the Traditional-vs-Roth tax-timing explanation with a concrete numeric example.

assessments.title: Major Purchases & Life-Admin Systems - Mastery Check

questions.prompt: Someone is evaluating whether to take a $3,000 trip, financed on a credit card at 22% APR and paid off over 8 months (adding ~$250 in interest). What does a structured cost-benefit evaluation require beyond the $3,000 sticker price?
options: A major purchase should be evaluated on sticker price alone.
Whether friends or family approve of the purchase.
True total cost (including financing interest), the opportunity cost of that money elsewhere, and alignment with the person's existing budget and priorities.
A young, healthy person doesn't need a will or power of attorney.

optionFeedback.feedback: This assumes a young, healthy person has no need for a will or power of attorney, but incapacity or death can occur unexpectedly at any age.
The framework is about personal financial alignment and total cost, not external approval.
Correct - as shown in the worked example, the true cost is closer to $3,250, and the decision should also weigh alternatives and priority fit.
This treats sticker price as the only factor, but financing interest and opportunity cost meaningfully change the real cost of the decision.

successCriteria: Learner selects the correct option and can restate why each distractor is wrong.

rubric.excellent: Selects the correct option on the first attempt.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct option after reviewing feedback on an initial incorrect attempt.
rubric.weak: Requires multiple attempts and cannot restate why the correct option is right afterward.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects the distractor reflecting: A young, healthy person doesn't need a will or power of attorney.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the worked example addressing 'A young, healthy person doesn't need a will or power of attorney.' before allowing a retry.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects the distractor reflecting: A major purchase should be evaluated on sticker price alone.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the worked example addressing 'A major purchase should be evaluated on sticker price alone.' before allowing a retry.

questions.prompt: A city government is deciding whether to fund a costly infrastructure upgrade upfront (higher initial cost, lower long-run cost) versus ongoing patch repairs (lower initial cost, higher long-run cost). Apply the major-purchase total-cost-and-alternatives framework to this public-policy decision.
successCriteria: Response correctly applies the core reasoning to the specific scenario given, not just a restatement of the general rule.
Response reaches a concrete, specific conclusion rather than a vague generality.

rubric.excellent: Explicitly compares total cost over a defined horizon for both options (not just upfront cost), considers opportunity cost of capital tied up either way, and reaches a horizon-dependent recommendation rather than assuming the cheaper upfront option is better.
rubric.adequate: Recommends one option with some cost reasoning but doesn't fully compare total cost over a horizon.
rubric.weak: Recommends the cheaper upfront option without considering long-run cost differences.

errorPatterns.description: Learner evaluates a major decision on upfront/sticker cost alone without considering total cost over time.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the $3,000 trip worked example and have the learner rebuild an equivalent total-cost table for the infrastructure decision.

assessments.title: Processing Rejection & Setbacks - Mastery Check

questions.prompt: After several job application rejections in a row, what does a resilience-reframing approach suggest?
options: Stop applying until confidence fully returns.
Reframe the rejections as an expected part of a numbers-driven process, extract feedback where possible, and keep applying - while still allowing the disappointment to be real.
Rejection at this life stage reflects personal inadequacy.
Resilience means not feeling upset by setbacks.

optionFeedback.feedback: The framework emphasizes continuing to act despite difficult feelings, not waiting for the feelings to resolve first.
Correct - this combines the attribution, numbers-game, and extract-feedback reframes without requiring the feelings to disappear first.
This treats rejection as a personal verdict, but it's typically a numbers-driven outcome of a competitive process, not a reflection of inadequacy.
This equates resilience with not feeling upset, but resilience means continuing to act despite difficult feelings, not suppressing them.

successCriteria: Learner selects the correct option and can restate why each distractor is wrong.

rubric.excellent: Selects the correct option on the first attempt.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct option after reviewing feedback on an initial incorrect attempt.
rubric.weak: Requires multiple attempts and cannot restate why the correct option is right afterward.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects the distractor reflecting: Rejection at this life stage reflects personal inadequacy.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the worked example addressing 'Rejection at this life stage reflects personal inadequacy.' before allowing a retry.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects the distractor reflecting: Resilience means not feeling upset by setbacks.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the worked example addressing 'Resilience means not feeling upset by setbacks.' before allowing a retry.

questions.prompt: A product team's feature launch underperforms and the team is discouraged. Apply the resilience-reframing logic (attribution reframe, numbers-game reframe, extract-feedback reframe) to this professional, team-level setback rather than a personal one.
successCriteria: Response correctly applies the core reasoning to the specific scenario given, not just a restatement of the general rule.
Response reaches a concrete, specific conclusion rather than a vague generality.

rubric.excellent: Applies all three reframes explicitly: attributes the underperformance to factors beyond simple team failure (market timing, discovery process), frames launches as a numbers-driven iteration process where not every attempt succeeds, and identifies concrete feedback to extract from the launch data - while acknowledging the team's disappointment is valid.
rubric.adequate: Applies one or two of the reframes but not all three, or doesn't acknowledge the team's real disappointment.
rubric.weak: Frames the underperformance purely as a team failure, or dismisses the team's discouragement as something to just get over.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats a setback as a definitive judgment of competence rather than an expected part of a numbers-driven process.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the job-rejection worked example's three reframes and apply them explicitly to this new scenario.

glossary.definition: A plan that assigns a job to every dollar of income before you spend it.
A cost that stays the same each month, such as rent or a car payment.
A cost that changes month to month, such as groceries or gas.
A budgeting framework allocating roughly 50% of after-tax income to needs, 30% to wants, and 20% to savings/debt paydown.
A bank account designed for frequent transactions such as bill pay and debit card use, typically earning little to no interest.
A bank account designed to hold money not being spent immediately, earning modest interest.
A savings account, usually at an online bank, paying a significantly higher interest rate than a standard savings account.
The real rate of return an account pays over one year, including the effect of compounding.
Interest calculated on both the original amount and the interest already earned, so growth accelerates over time.
Cash set aside specifically to cover unexpected expenses or income loss, kept liquid rather than invested.
A structure for difficult conversations: describe the specific situation, explain its impact, then make a clear request.
Stating your needs clearly and respectfully, without minimizing them or attacking the other person.
Avoiding stating your needs directly, often leading to resentment or the issue never being addressed.
Stating your needs in a way that blames, threatens, or disrespects the other person.
The tendency for the first number stated in a negotiation to set the reference point the rest of the discussion revolves around.

glossary.term: BATNA (Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement)
glossary.definition: Your fallback option if a negotiation fails; knowing it gives you a real walk-away point.
The specific terms below (or above) which you will end a negotiation rather than accept a deal.
Conceding on a lower-priority term in exchange for gaining ground on a higher-priority one.
A three-digit number, commonly 300-850, summarizing creditworthiness, used by lenders to set loan terms.
The detailed record of credit accounts, payment history, and inquiries that a credit score is calculated from.
The percentage of total available credit currently in use (balance divided by credit limit).
A lender-initiated credit check, such as a loan application, which can slightly lower a credit score.
A self-check or pre-qualification credit check that does not affect the credit score.
The yearly cost of borrowing, including interest, expressed as a percentage.
Paying off debts in order of highest interest rate first, minimizing total interest paid.
Paying off debts in order of smallest balance first, prioritizing psychological momentum.
A short-term financing option that splits a purchase into installments, often marketed as interest-free.
A deliberately confusing or frustrating design choice, such as a hidden cancel button, intended to discourage an action.
Total income before taxes and deductions.
Income remaining after subtracting deductions, which tax is actually calculated on.
The tax rate applied to the next dollar of income earned, based on which bracket it falls into.
Total tax paid divided by total income; the real, blended rate actually paid.
A range of income taxed at a specific marginal rate under a progressive tax system.
Tax an employer takes out of each paycheck in advance, estimating annual liability.
A fixed dollar amount subtracted from taxable income, available to all filers who don't itemize.
An amount subtracted directly from the tax owed, rather than from taxable income.
The category, such as single or married filing jointly, that determines tax brackets and standard deduction.
The process of moving a dispute to a higher level of authority when the first response is unsatisfactory.
The amount paid, usually monthly, to keep an insurance policy active regardless of use.
The amount paid out-of-pocket before insurance starts covering costs.
A fixed dollar amount paid for a specific service, such as a doctor visit.
A percentage of a cost the insured is responsible for after the deductible is met.
The most an insured person pays in a plan year before insurance covers 100% of further costs.
Whether a provider has a negotiated rate agreement with an insurer (in-network) or not (out-of-network, typically more expensive).
A statement from an insurer showing what was billed, covered, and owed; it is not a bill.
A determination by an insurer that a specific charge won't be covered.
A general doctor who serves as the main point of contact for most health needs.
The upfront cash paid toward a home purchase, reducing the mortgage amount.
Fees paid at the time a home purchase is finalized, typically 2-5% of the purchase price.
Insurance required by lenders when a down payment is under 20%, protecting the lender if the loan defaults.
Total monthly debt payments divided by gross monthly income, used by lenders to assess affordability.
A recurring fee some properties charge for shared amenities and maintenance.
Money held by a landlord to cover potential damage or unpaid rent, generally refundable minus itemized deductions.
A legal standard requiring a rental unit to meet basic living conditions such as working plumbing, heat, and structural safety.
Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning; a home's climate control system.
Routine upkeep performed before a problem occurs, to avoid larger failures later.
Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter; a safety outlet, usually near water, that cuts power if it detects a current leak.
A switch that automatically interrupts electrical flow when a circuit is overloaded, preventing fires.
A structured approach to unfamiliar problems: research possible causes, attempt a safe fix within skill/legal limits, escalate to a professional if it persists.
Repairs, often electrical, gas, or structural, that legally require a licensed professional and/or a permit in most jurisdictions.
The full cost of owning an asset, including purchase price, financing, insurance, maintenance, and depreciation.
The loss in an asset's value over time.
The length of time over which a loan is repaid.

Three separate contractor protections: legal permission to do the work, a bond protecting the client financially if the work isn't completed, and insurance covering accidents or damage during the work.
A written document specifying exactly what work will be done, materials used, and timeline.
A formal written amendment to the scope of work and price, used when project requirements change mid-job.
An employer-sponsored retirement account, often with an employer matching contribution.
Money an employer contributes to a retirement account, typically as a percentage match of the employee's own contribution up to a limit.
A retirement account funded with pre-tax dollars; withdrawals in retirement are taxed.
A retirement account funded with after-tax dollars; qualified withdrawals in retirement are tax-free.
The schedule by which employer-matched retirement contributions become fully the employee's own over time.
A legal document authorizing someone to make decisions on another's behalf if they're unable to.
A legal document specifying how a person's assets should be distributed and, if applicable, who should care for dependents.
The value of the next-best alternative given up when making a choice.
Deliberately interpreting a situation through a different, more accurate or useful lens.
